project.name: ahnafnafee.dev
project.description: An SEO-optimized personal portfolio and MDX blog built with Next.js 16 (App Router), React 19, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS v4. Blog, portfolio, and research content are driven from on-disk MDX files with connected @graph JSON-LD schema, dynamic OG images, and an llms.txt manifest. Supports both Vercel deployment and a static export.

nodes.languageNotes: Cron-triggered with cancel-in-progress disabled to avoid clobbering an in-flight quota run; installs tsx and googleapis ad hoc with --legacy-peer-deps.

nodes.name: notify-search-engines.yml

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what this project is: an SEO-optimized personal portfolio and MDX blog built on Next.js 16 (App Router), React 19, TypeScript, and Tailwind v4. The README's central thesis — every page emits a single connected schema.org @graph, OG images are normalized to 1200x630, and llms.txt is regenerated on every build — is the story the rest of this tour walks through in code. The package.json sits alongside it as the project's script catalog (dev, build, export, lint, test, sitemap, indexing), so you can see at a glance how the moving parts are wired together.

tour.title: Application Entry Point
tour.description: Next.js App Router has no single classic main() file; the natural reading order is the root layout followed by the home page. layout.tsx is the global shell — it wires up fonts, the theme provider, the site header, the toaster, analytics, and injects the WebSite + SiteNavigationElement JSON-LD that powers Google sitelinks. page.tsx is the home React Server Component that aggregates the latest blog post, featured portfolio projects, and featured research, batches their view counts, and emits the canonical Person JSON-LD graph. These two files are the seams where every later layer plugs in.
tour.languageLesson: In the Next.js App Router, layout.tsx and page.tsx are file-system conventions, not imports — the framework composes layout (persistent shell) around page (route content) automatically. Both default to React Server Components, so data fetching happens on the server with no client-side bundle cost unless a child opts in with 'use client'.

tour.title: The Content Type System
tour.description: Before reading the content pipeline, understand the contract it enforces. This ambient declaration file defines a `me` module with the frontmatter shapes — Blog, Portfolio, and Research (plus its Author, Affiliation, Venue, and Links sub-types) — that every MDX file on disk must satisfy. Because the whole site is MDX-on-disk with no CMS, these types are the single source of truth: add a new content field here first, then update the MDX files, and TypeScript propagates the change through every reader and page.
tour.languageLesson: A `.d.ts` ambient declaration with `declare module 'me'` lets the project import strongly-typed frontmatter as if 'me' were a real package, even though the shapes are only ever produced at runtime by gray-matter. This is TypeScript's way of putting a static contract over dynamic, file-derived data.

tour.title: The Content Pipeline: Readers
tour.description: This is the architectural heart of the site. Three readers parse MDX off disk: getContents returns full entries (header + body) for listing pages and generateStaticParams, getContentBySlug resolves one entry for a detail page, and getContentHeaders returns frontmatter only — skipping body parsing for performance whenever a page just needs metadata. All three resolve paths through LOCATION_DIR (src/data), so content location is changed in one place, not in the readers. Building on the Step 3 types, gray-matter parses each file's frontmatter into the typed shapes the rest of the app consumes.

tour.title: Rendering MDX on a Page
tour.description: With content read off disk, the blog detail page renders it. blog/[slug]/page.tsx statically generates a route per post, builds per-post SEO metadata and JSON-LD, and feeds the MDX body to next-mdx-remote. mdxConfig.ts centralizes the plugin set — remark-gfm, remark-math, rehype-prism-plus, rehype-slug, rehype-katex — shared by every content renderer, so blog, portfolio, and research all transform MDX identically. This is where the readers from Step 4 meet the components in Step 6.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js dynamic routes use a `[slug]` folder name; `generateStaticParams` enumerates every slug at build time so the framework can pre-render each post to static HTML. Combined with `next-mdx-remote/rsc`, the MDX is compiled on the server — no MDX runtime ships to the browser.

tour.title: Custom MDX Components
tour.description: MDX lets authors write Markdown that renders as React. The index.ts barrel assembles the MDXComponents map that overrides default HTML tags with bespoke components: Pre detects the code language and routes ```mermaid``` fences to a lazy diagram renderer, Mermaid loads from a CDN and renders theme-aware SVG with a fullscreen viewer, ContentImage handles ImageKit-hosted images with a click-to-zoom lightbox, and TLDR renders the front-loaded summary callout every blog post opens with. This map is what makes the authoring conventions in the README possible.

tour.title: SEO: The Connected @graph
tour.description: This is the feature the README leads with: every page emits one connected schema.org @graph rather than scattered snippets. personSchema.ts defines the canonical Person node with a stable `@id` that author and publisher references across the site point to — the full node is emitted only on the home page (Step 2), and every other page references it by id. faqSchema.ts and howToSchema.ts build FAQPage and HowTo rich-result JSON-LD from MDX-authored data. Together they turn ordinary content into structured data search engines and LLMs can cite.
tour.languageLesson: schema.org's `@graph` with shared `@id` references lets multiple JSON-LD nodes form one entity graph instead of duplicating the same Person on every page. Emitting the full node once and referencing it by `@id` elsewhere keeps the structured data DRY and tells crawlers these are all the same real-world entity.

tour.title: OG Image Generation
tour.description: When a post has no thumbnail, the site generates a 1200x630 Open Graph card on the fly. metapage/ogImage.ts builds the /api/og URL from a payload, sanitizing topic chips and appending a deploy-SHA cache-buster. The /api/og edge route parses those params, loads fonts, and renders a PNG ImageResponse — choosing the home card or the generic card built by og-shell.tsx. og-tokens.ts is the shared design-token module (canvas size, fonts, per-page-type accent palettes) that keeps every generated card visually consistent with the site.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js `ImageResponse` (the @vercel/og runtime) renders JSX to a PNG at the edge, so OG cards are generated dynamically per-request without a headless browser. Running on the Edge runtime means cold starts are near-instant, but only a subset of Node APIs and a JSX-to-image layout engine (flexbox-only) are available.

tour.title: Design System & Theme Bridge
tour.description: The visual layer bridges two systems. globals.css defines the shadcn semantic OKLCH tokens for light/dark themes and intentionally maps them onto the legacy Tailwind palette retained in tailwind.config.js (primary=blue, theme=neutral, a custom gray ramp) — so `bg-primary` and `bg-primary-500` render the same color during the transition. cn() (lib/utils.ts) and twclsx.ts merge conditional classes through tailwind-merge to resolve conflicts, and button.tsx is the representative shadcn/ui primitive: cva-based variants imported across the component layer.
tour.languageLesson: Tailwind v4 reads its config from `@config` inside CSS, and `@theme inline` lets you define design tokens as CSS custom properties. Mapping shadcn semantic tokens (--primary, --background) to the same OKLCH values as a legacy numeric palette is how a project migrates design systems without a flag-day rewrite — both class styles stay valid simultaneously.

tour.title: Pageview Counts: Service & Data
tour.description: View counts are the site's only piece of persistent state. The pageviews service reads a single slug's count, batch-reads many slugs for listing pages, and atomically upserts/increments on a view. It talks to Neon serverless Postgres through neon.ts, which lazily memoizes the SQL client and degrades gracefully (returning null) when no connection string is set. The migration SQL defines the minimal one-row-per-slug schema that backs it — deliberately tiny so it fits Neon's free tier.
tour.languageLesson: SQL upserts via `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE` make an increment atomic in a single round-trip, avoiding a read-then-write race under concurrent views. The schema stays intentionally denormalized (one counter row per route) because the access pattern is a pure key-value increment, not relational querying.

tour.title: API Routes: Views & Revalidation
tour.description: Two serverless route handlers expose the dynamic behavior. The pageviews route uses GET to read a count and POST to increment it, calling the Step 10 service. The revalidate route validates a secret query param and calls revalidatePath to purge a single slug's ISR cache on demand — this is how a freshly-edited post goes live without a full redeploy. Note these routes are exactly what the static-export pipeline in Step 12 has to work around.
tour.languageLesson: In the App Router, a `route.ts` file exporting named functions (GET, POST) defines an API endpoint; the HTTP method maps to the export name. `revalidatePath` is Next.js's On-Demand Incremental Static Regeneration primitive — it invalidates a cached route so the next request rebuilds it, giving static-speed pages with CMS-like freshness.

tour.title: Build & Static-Export Tooling
tour.description: The project supports two build modes, and this tooling makes the static one possible. Because Next.js can't static-export a tree containing API routes, export-prepare.js moves src/app/api into a backup before the export and export-cleanup.js restores it after — bracketing the build. custom-next-sitemap.js runs in postbuild, reading MDX frontmatter for lastmod dates, assigning per-route priorities, and allowlisting AI/LLM crawlers. generate-llms-txt.js regenerates the llms.txt manifest from frontmatter so AI crawlers can discover the site's canonical content and APIs.

tour.title: CI/CD & Search-Engine Indexing
tour.description: The capstone ties everything to automation. ci.yml runs on every push and PR — type-check, lint, the Vitest suite, a Next.js build, JSON-LD validation, and a warn-only alt-text audit — gating the quality of every layer this tour covered. notify-search-engines.yml fires when MDX content lands on main, waits for the Vercel deploy, computes the changed files, and submits the new URLs to IndexNow, Google, and Baidu via notify-search-engines.js. google-batch-reindex.yml runs weekly to catch any URLs that slipped through, and sendIndexingRequest.ts is the manual companion for batch submission. This is the engine that turns the SEO work from Steps 7-8 into actual search and AI visibility.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions `on:` triggers fan out to multiple events (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch). A content-publish workflow that waits for the deploy, diffs changed files, and pings search engines is event-driven SEO automation — the repo, not a human, keeps the search index fresh.
